H.name: Hydrogen
kid_facts: Most common element; stars are mostly hydrogen.

He.name: Helium
kid_facts: Helium makes balloons float and doesn’t burn.

Li.name: Lithium
kid_facts: Lithium is used in rechargeable batteries.

Be.name: Beryllium
kid_facts: Beryllium helps make emeralds (beryl).

B.name: Boron
kid_facts: Boron makes strong glass and borax.

C.name: Carbon
kid_facts: Carbon forms diamond and graphite—and life.

N.name: Nitrogen
kid_facts: Nitrogen is 78% of Earth’s air.

O.name: Oxygen
kid_facts: Oxygen helps you breathe and fuels fires.

F.name: Fluorine
kid_facts: Fluorine is extremely reactive; in toothpaste as fluoride compounds.

Ne.name: Neon
kid_facts: Neon glows bright in signs.

Na.name: Sodium
kid_facts: Sodium + chlorine = table salt.

Mg.name: Magnesium
kid_facts: Magnesium burns with a bright white flame.

Al.name: Aluminium
kid_facts: Aluminum is light; used in planes and cans.

Si.name: Silicon
kid_facts: Silicon is the base of computer chips.

P.name: Phosphorus
kid_facts: Phosphorus can glow faintly in the dark.

S.name: Sulfur
kid_facts: Sulfur can smell like rotten eggs when burned.

Cl.name: Chlorine
kid_facts: Chlorine keeps pools clean (in compounds).

Ar.name: Argon
kid_facts: Argon protects hot filaments in bulbs.

K.name: Potassium
kid_facts: Potassium is important for nerves in your body.

Ca.name: Calcium
kid_facts: Calcium builds strong bones and teeth.

Sc.name: Scandium
kid_facts: Scandium is used in bright lights and alloys.

Ti.name: Titanium
kid_facts: Titanium is strong and light; used in jets.

V.name: Vanadium
kid_facts: Vanadium strengthens steel.

Cr.name: Chromium
kid_facts: Chromium makes stainless steel shiny.

Mn.name: Manganese
kid_facts: Manganese helps make steel tough.

Fe.name: Iron
kid_facts: Iron carries oxygen in your blood (hemoglobin).

Co.name: Cobalt
kid_facts: Cobalt colors glass blue and powers batteries.

Ni.name: Nickel
kid_facts: Nickel is found in many coins and alloys.

Cu.name: Copper
kid_facts: Copper is great for electrical wires.

Zn.name: Zinc
kid_facts: Zinc protects steel from rusting (galvanizing).

Ga.name: Gallium
kid_facts: Gallium can melt in your hand (low melting point).

Ge.name: Germanium
kid_facts: Germanium was used in early transistors.

As.name: Arsenic
kid_facts: Arsenic is poisonous; used carefully in industry.

Se.name: Selenium
kid_facts: Selenium helps solar cells and photocopiers.

Br.name: Bromine
kid_facts: Bromine is a red-brown liquid at room temp.

Kr.name: Krypton
kid_facts: Krypton is used in special high-speed flashes.

Rb.name: Rubidium
kid_facts: Rubidium reacts violently with water.

Sr.name: Strontium
kid_facts: Strontium gives red color to fireworks.

Y.name: Yttrium
kid_facts: Yttrium is used in LEDs and lasers.

Zr.name: Zirconium
kid_facts: Zirconium resists corrosion; used in reactors.

Nb.name: Niobium
kid_facts: Niobium makes superconducting magnets.

Mo.name: Molybdenum
kid_facts: Molybdenum raises steel’s strength at high temps.

Tc.name: Technetium
kid_facts: Technetium is radioactive; used in medical imaging.

Ru.name: Ruthenium
kid_facts: Ruthenium hardens platinum and palladium.

Rh.name: Rhodium
kid_facts: Rhodium helps clean car exhaust (catalytic converters).

Pd.name: Palladium
kid_facts: Palladium is used in electronics and catalysts.

Ag.name: Silver
kid_facts: Silver conducts electricity very well.

Cd.name: Cadmium
kid_facts: Cadmium used in batteries; must be handled safely.

In.name: Indium
kid_facts: Indium makes touchscreens work (ITO).

Sn.name: Tin
kid_facts: Tin protects steel from rust (tin cans).

Sb.name: Antimony
kid_facts: Antimony is used in flame retardants.

Te.name: Tellurium
kid_facts: Tellurium helps solar panels (CdTe).

I.name: Iodine
kid_facts: Iodine is added to salt for thyroid health.

Xe.name: Xenon
kid_facts: Xenon goes into bright car headlights.

Cs.name: Cesium
kid_facts: Cesium clocks are super accurate.

Ba.name: Barium
kid_facts: Barium makes X‑ray images clearer (contrast).

La.name: Lanthanum
kid_facts: Lanthanum is used in camera lenses.

Ce.name: Cerium
kid_facts: Cerium helps catalytic converters and glass polishing.

Pr.name: Praseodymium
kid_facts: Praseodymium colors glass and magnets.

Nd.name: Neodymium
kid_facts: Neodymium makes very strong magnets.

Pm.name: Promethium
kid_facts: Promethium is radioactive; used in glow-in-the-dark paint historically.

Sm.name: Samarium
kid_facts: Samarium magnets resist demagnetizing.

Eu.name: Europium
kid_facts: Europium makes red in TV and LED screens.

Gd.name: Gadolinium
kid_facts: Gadolinium helps MRI contrast.

Tb.name: Terbium
kid_facts: Terbium makes green phosphors in screens.

Dy.name: Dysprosium
kid_facts: Dysprosium helps magnets stay strong at heat.

Ho.name: Holmium
kid_facts: Holmium has the highest magnetic strength.

Er.name: Erbium
kid_facts: Erbium colors glass pink; used in fiber optics.

Tm.name: Thulium
kid_facts: Thulium is rare; used in lasers.

Yb.name: Ytterbium
kid_facts: Ytterbium helps fiber laser technology.

Lu.name: Lutetium
kid_facts: Lutetium is used in PET scan detectors.

Hf.name: Hafnium
kid_facts: Hafnium is used in superalloys and chips.

Ta.name: Tantalum
kid_facts: Tantalum is used in capacitors and phones.

W.name: Tungsten
kid_facts: Tungsten has the highest melting point of metals.

Re.name: Rhenium
kid_facts: Rhenium is used in jet engine alloys.

Os.name: Osmium
kid_facts: Osmium is very dense and hard.

Ir.name: Iridium
kid_facts: Iridium is very corrosion‑resistant.

Pt.name: Platinum
kid_facts: Platinum is a catalyst and jewelry metal.

Au.name: Gold
kid_facts: Gold does not tarnish and conducts well.

Hg.name: Mercury
kid_facts: Mercury is a liquid metal at room temp.

Tl.name: Thallium
kid_facts: Thallium compounds must be handled carefully (toxic).

Pb.name: Lead
kid_facts: Lead is heavy; used in shielding; toxic if ingested.

Bi.name: Bismuth
kid_facts: Bismuth is heavy but less toxic than lead.

Po.name: Polonium
kid_facts: Polonium is highly radioactive.

At.name: Astatine
kid_facts: Astatine is extremely rare and radioactive.

Rn.name: Radon
kid_facts: Radon is a radioactive gas found in some basements.

Fr.name: Francium
kid_facts: Francium is extremely rare and short‑lived.

Ra.name: Radium
kid_facts: Radium glows faintly; it’s radioactive.

Ac.name: Actinium
kid_facts: Actinium is radioactive and glows in the dark.

Th.name: Thorium
kid_facts: Thorium may power future reactors (research).

Pa.name: Protactinium
kid_facts: Protactinium is very rare and radioactive.

U.name: Uranium
kid_facts: Uranium fuels nuclear reactors.

Np.name: Neptunium
kid_facts: Neptunium is synthetic and radioactive.

Pu.name: Plutonium
kid_facts: Plutonium is used in nuclear energy and research.

Am.name: Americium
kid_facts: Americium is in smoke detectors.

Cm.name: Curium
kid_facts: Curium is highly radioactive; named for the Curies.

Bk.name: Berkelium
kid_facts: Berkelium was discovered in Berkeley, California.

Cf.name: Californium
kid_facts: Californium can help start nuclear reactors.

Es.name: Einsteinium
kid_facts: Einsteinium is named after Albert Einstein.

Fm.name: Fermium
kid_facts: Fermium is made in nuclear explosions.

Md.name: Mendelevium
kid_facts: Mendelevium honors Dmitri Mendeleev.

No.name: Nobelium
kid_facts: Nobelium honors Alfred Nobel.

Lr.name: Lawrencium
kid_facts: Lawrencium honors Ernest Lawrence.

Rf.name: Rutherfordium
kid_facts: Rutherfordium honors Ernest Rutherford.

Db.name: Dubnium
kid_facts: Dubnium is named after Dubna, Russia.

Sg.name: Seaborgium
kid_facts: Seaborgium honors Glenn Seaborg.

Bh.name: Bohrium
kid_facts: Bohrium honors Niels Bohr.

Hs.name: Hassium
kid_facts: Hassium is named after Hesse in Germany.

Mt.name: Meitnerium
kid_facts: Meitnerium honors Lise Meitner.

Ds.name: Darmstadtium
kid_facts: Darmstadtium is named after Darmstadt, Germany.

Rg.name: Roentgenium
kid_facts: Roentgenium honors Wilhelm Röntgen.

Cn.name: Copernicium
kid_facts: Copernicium honors Nicolaus Copernicus.

Nh.name: Nihonium
kid_facts: Nihonium means “Japan” in Japanese.

Fl.name: Flerovium
kid_facts: Flerovium honors Flerov Laboratory in Russia.

Mc.name: Moscovium
kid_facts: Moscovium honors Moscow region.

Lv.name: Livermorium
kid_facts: Livermorium honors Livermore, California.

Ts.name: Tennessine
kid_facts: Tennessine honors Tennessee, USA.

Og.name: Oganesson
kid_facts: Oganesson honors Yuri Oganessian; very short‑lived.

H2.name: Hydrogen gas
kid_facts: Super light gas; rockets use it.

O2.name: Oxygen gas
kid_facts: About 21% of air is oxygen.

N2.name: Nitrogen gas
kid_facts: 78% of air is nitrogen.

H2O.name: Water
kid_facts: Water has a bent shape.

CO2.name: Carbon dioxide
kid_facts: Plants use CO₂ and make oxygen.

CH4.name: Methane
kid_facts: Main part of natural gas.

NH3.name: Ammonia
kid_facts: Strong smell; used in cleaners.
